note_data.title: Forces, density and pressure
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in a sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
Angular speed (ω) can be calculated using ω = v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is time period, and f is frequency
An object moving in circular motion at constant speed has constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, therefore it must be accelerating
Centripetal force always acts perpendicular to the motion of the object towards the centre of the circle
Centripetal acceleration is given by a = v²/r = ω²r, where v is linear speed, r is radius, and ω is angular speed
Centripetal force is calculated using F = mv²/r = mω²r, derived from Newton's second law
A gravitational field is an area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
Gravitational field strength (g) is the force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field, calculated using g = F/m
Newton's law of gravitation states that gravitational force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them: F = Gm₁m₂/r²

definitions.definition: The angle in a sector of a circle when the arc length of that sector is equal to the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The acceleration experienced by an object moving in a circular path due to constantly changing velocity direction
The force that acts perpendicular to the motion of an object moving in a circle, always directed towards the centre
An area where objects with mass experience a non-contact force
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give exact definition with correct physics terminology
Show working clearly with correct units and significant figures

modelAnswer.question: A block of wood of volume 0.40 m³ floats in water with 0.30 m³ submerged. Calculate the density of the wood. (Density of water = 1000 kg m⁻³)
modelAnswer.answer: For floating object, weight = upthrust. Weight of wood = mg = ρwood × Vwood × g. Upthrust = weight of water displaced = ρwater × Vsubmerged × g. Therefore: ρwood × 0.40 × g = 1000 × 0.30 × g. ρwood = (1000 × 0.30)/0.40 = 750 kg m⁻³
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Weight = upthrust for floating object

commonMistakes: Thinking that an object moving at constant speed in a circle is not accelerating - it is accelerating because velocity direction is constantly changing
Confusing centripetal force direction - it always acts towards the centre of the circle, not outwards
Forgetting to convert degrees to radians when using circular motion equations - multiply degrees by π/180
Assuming gravitational field strength is the same everywhere - it varies in radial fields but is constant in uniform fields
examinerTips: Remember that a complete circle is 2π radians, so use the conversion factor π/180 to convert from degrees to radians
When calculating gravitational forces, treat uniform spheres as point masses acting at their centre for objects outside the sphere
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Force diagram showing object in fluid with upthrust arrow pointing upward and weight arrow pointing downward, plus moment diagram showing forces and perpendicular distances from pivot point
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate the pressure at the bottom of a water tank and explain why pressure increases with depth
In O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on density calculation using mass and volume measurements
Featured in M/J 2022 P2 Q6 (8 marks) — students had to explain how hydraulic systems work using Pascal's principle and calculate force multiplication
Appeared in O/N 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — question on measuring density of irregular objects using displacement method and identifying sources of error

workedExample.question: A hydraulic jack has a small piston of area 20 cm² and a large piston of area 500 cm². A force of 100 N is applied to the small piston. Calculate the force produced by the large piston. [4 marks]
steps: Step 1: State Pascal's principle - pressure is transmitted equally throughout a fluid
Step 2: Write the pressure equation: P₁ = P₂, so F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂
Step 3: Substitute values: 100 N / 20 cm² = F₂ / 500 cm²
Step 4: Solve for F₂: F₂ = (100 × 500) / 20 = 2500 N
workedExample.answer: The force produced by the large piston is 2500 N. This demonstrates force multiplication in hydraulic systems.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for stating Pascal's principle, 1 mark for correct equation, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for correct final answer with unit

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused mass and weight when calculating density' — always use mass (kg) not weight (N) in density calculations
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to convert units before calculations' — always check cm² to m² and cm³ to m³ conversions in pressure problems
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often stated pressure increases with depth but failed to explain why' — always mention that fluid weight above creates the pressure when explaining depth effects
note_data.keyDiagram: Essential diagram: Hydraulic system showing two connected pistons of different areas in a U-shaped container filled with liquid. Label: small piston (A₁), large piston (A₂), applied force F₁, output force F₂, and liquid level. Include arrows showing force directions and indicate that liquid pressure is equal at the same horizontal level. This diagram demonstrates Pascal's principle and force multiplication.
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion involves angular displacement measured in radians and angular speed related to linear speed by ω = v/r. Objects in circular motion experience centripetal acceleration (a = v²/r) requiring centripetal force (F = mv²/r) directed towards the centre. Gravitational fields exert forces on masses, with field strength g = F/m, following Newton's law of gravitation F = Gm₁m₂/r².
